q: What are some of the pain points facing customers in the payments, money transfer, and foreign exchange businesses?
lead: Customers face payments that are slow, non-transparent, and costly, especially across borders, alongside poor customer service and many hidden fees from existing intermediaries.
points: A bank wire costs around $35 and takes several business days, even domestically.
Overseas remittances can cost around 10% and take weeks to arrive.
FX exchanges routinely charge 3% or more, often hidden as 'zero commission'.
Incumbents provide poor service and charge many fees with little transparency.

q: How important are payments for banks and what are the different sources of revenue?
lead: Payments are highly important, generating close to 40% of bank revenues from fees and interest charges, with global revenues of US$1.9 trillion in 2020.
points: North America payments earned $485 billion in 2020, 44% from credit card interest and fees.
28% domestic payment fees, 20% account/overdraft interest, 9% cross-border fees.
JPMorgan: 47 million Chase customers made 69 fee-generating transactions monthly.
65% of clients would leave a firm lacking integrated digital channels.

q: What are the payment rails and what is the difference between the large value transfer system and the system for small payments?
lead: Payment rails are the physical network of cables and computers routing electronic payments; the LVTS handles large business payments fast, while small-payment systems handle high-volume consumer payments slowly.
points: LVTS transfers large sums between banks in one day or intraday, enabling real-time gross settlement.
US LVTS: Fedwire and CHIPS; Canada: Lynx; Europe: TARGET2.
Small payments use ACH (US), ACSS (Canada), EUCHA (Europe) for checks and cards.
Small-payment 'local roads' are slower, taking days to complete.

q: Who are the players in the payments ecosystem and what roles do they play?
lead: Players include the customer, issuing bank, credit card network, merchant bank (acquirer), payment gateway, and payment processor, plus banks and money service businesses.
points: Issuing bank approves the card, lends funds, and bills the customer.
Card networks (VISA, MasterCard, Amex) set rules and interchange fees.
Merchant bank/acquirer provides the POS machine and receives merchant funds.
Payment gateway securely transmits data; processor routes messages between gateway and banks.

q: What are the sources of revenue for payment and money transfer companies? Which revenues are fee-based and which are risk-based?
lead: Payment players earn fee-based revenue from interchange, assessment, processing, and conversion fees, and risk-based income from taking FX, liquidity, and credit risk exposures.
points: Fee-based: interchange (1.75%), assessment/swipe fee (0.11%), processing fee, cross-border conversion up to 3%.
Risk-based: FX risk earns the bid-ask spread on currency inventories.
Risk-based: liquidity risk from depositing collateral; credit risk from potential customer default.
Banks also charge indirect fees: account, maintenance, NSF, and trade finance fees.

q: What has been the strategy for new entrants into payments and how did they benefit from technological and regulatory changes?
lead: New entrants started with a single use-case, built a foothold with core customers, then expanded the product offering to capture more of the value chain and reach mainstream customers.
points: Single use-cases: mobile money, fair FX rates, or quick, inexpensive remittances.
Benefited from internet, smartphones, 3G/4G/5G networks, and cloud computing.
Benefited from real-time gross settlement, NFC, tap-and-go, QR codes, and blockchain.
Regulatory changes promoting open banking, open APIs, and opening the payment rails.

q: What are examples of pain points in payments and the fintechs that solved them?
lead: Fintechs solved pain points in e-commerce trust, money transfers, foreign exchange, remittances, and cashless payment, including Alipay, M-PESA, Wise, WorldRemit, and Octopus.
points: Alipay created trust in e-commerce via escrow accounts releasing funds only on delivery.
M-PESA democratized money transfers for unbanked Kenyans using mobile phones.
Wise lowered FX costs with mid-market rates and transparent flat fees.
WorldRemit cut remittance costs to a $3.99 fee and 1% conversion rate.

q: What are the different segments of the insurance market and how can we assess their relative size?
lead: Insurance is a US$5.7 trillion (2020) industry measured by gross written premiums, split into life (42%), property and casualty (31%), and health (27%) insurance.
points: Size is measured by gross written premiums, the dollar value of premiums paid.
Life insurance pays a lump sum on disability or premature death.
P&C covers property damage plus casualty risks like auto, theft, and liability.
Two B2B profit pools: distribution/policy management and reinsurance.

q: What are insurtechs and what has been their experience disrupting the insurance industry?
lead: Insurtechs are fintechs exploiting mobile apps, cloud, biometrics, sensors, data science, and AI to disrupt insurance; their experience has been rapid growth mixed with many failures.
points: Insurtechs raised close to $50 billion between 2018 and 2022, $17.8 billion in 2021.
CB Insights tracked 44 insurtech unicorns globally in 2022.
One-quarter of 760 sampled insurtechs shut down within 2019, often from lack of market appetite.
IPO performance was mixed: Lemonade +55% and Duck Creek +13%, but GoHealth -48% and Root -88%.

q: What are some examples of pain points solved by different insurtechs in customer-facing and back-office businesses?
lead: Customer-facing insurtechs personalize products and pricing while back-office insurtechs automate claims and administration to make insurance cheaper, faster, and more convenient.
points: Root customizes auto insurance using smartphone sensors to price driving behavior.
Lemonade uses AI bots Maya and Jim to sell policies and pay claims in seconds.
Cyence modeled cyber risk, acquired by Guidewire for $275 million in 2017.
Snapsheet processes auto claims by photo, estimating repairs in 2.7 hours on average.
